questions.question:
Given a computing system with two levels of cache (L1 and L2) and a main memory. The first level (L1) cache access time is 1 nanosecond (ns) and the hit rate for L1 cache is 90%, while the processor is accessing the data from L1 cache. Whereas, for the second level (L2) cache, the hit rate is 90% and the access time is 10 ns. The miss penalty for the data to be transferred from main memory to L2 cache is 100 ns.

Then the average memory access time in this system in nanoseconds is ________. (rounded off to one decimal place)
questions.to_find: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) of the two-level cache hierarchy in nanoseconds

understand.plain: A processor first checks L1 cache (1 ns, 90% hit rate). On a miss, it checks L2 cache (10 ns, 90% hit rate). If L2 also misses, it goes to main memory (100 ns penalty). We compute the weighted average time considering all levels.

keywords.explain: Fraction of memory accesses satisfied by a cache level without going to the next level.
keywords.example: L1 hit rate = 90% means 9 out of 10 accesses are found in L1.

keywords.explain: Extra time required to fetch data from a slower memory level on a cache miss.
keywords.example: If L2 misses, 100 ns is needed to fetch from main memory.

keywords.explain: Weighted average time for a memory access considering hit and miss rates at each level.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160' width='520' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='50' width='100' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L1 Cache</text><text x='60' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 ns, HR=90%</text><line x1='110' y1='80' x2='170' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='140' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>miss 10%</text><rect x='170' y='50' width='100' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L2 Cache</text><text x='220' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>10 ns, HR=90%</text><line x1='270' y1='80' x2='330' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='300' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>miss 10%</text><rect x='330' y='50' width='130' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Main Memory</text><text x='395' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>penalty 100 ns</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes connected in series: L1 Cache (1 ns, 90% hit) -> L2 Cache (10 ns, 90% hit) -> Main Memory (100 ns penalty). Arrows show 10% miss rate transitions.

given.aim: Compute the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a two-level cache hierarchy.

terms.meaning: L1 cache takes 1 ns; 90% of accesses are satisfied here.
terms.example: Out of 100 accesses, 90 finish in 1 ns.
terms.connects: Only 10% of accesses need to go to L2.

terms.meaning: L2 cache takes 10 ns; 90% of L1-miss accesses are found here.
terms.example: Of the 10 that miss L1, 9 are found in L2.
terms.connects: Only 1% of total accesses need to reach main memory.

terms.meaning: Time to transfer data from main memory to L2 on an L2 miss.
terms.example: The remaining 1 access (out of 100) incurs 100 ns.
terms.connects: This is the dominant cost when both caches miss.

given.plan: Use the hierarchical AMAT formula: AMAT = t_L1 + (1 - HR_L1) * [t_L2 + (1 - HR_L2) * t_MM]. Substitute the given values and compute.

steps.title: Write the AMAT formula
steps.note: The formula nests: L2 time is only paid on an L1 miss, and main memory time is only paid on both L1 and L2 miss.

steps.title: Compute final AMAT
steps.note: Rounded to one decimal place: 3.0 ns.

solution.result: The average memory access time is 3.0 ns.

questions.formula_note: Hierarchical AMAT formula: AMAT = t_L1 + (1 - HR_L1) * (t_L2 + (1 - HR_L2) * t_MM), where HR is hit rate at each level.

questions.question:
The main difference(s) between a CISC and a RISC processor is/are that a RISC processor typically

A. has fewer instructions
B. has fewer addressing modes
C. has more registers
D. is easier to implement using hardwired logic
questions.to_find: Which characteristics distinguish a RISC processor from a CISC processor

understand.plain: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) and CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) are two processor design philosophies. RISC simplifies the instruction set to allow faster execution, while CISC provides complex instructions. This question asks which listed traits are typical of RISC.

keywords.explain: Reduced Instruction Set Computer: uses a small set of simple, fixed-length instructions that execute in one clock cycle, with many registers.
keywords.example: ARM and MIPS processors are RISC architectures.

keywords.explain: Complex Instruction Set Computer: uses many complex instructions with variable lengths, fewer registers, and microcode-based control.
A control unit implemented with logic gates directly (no microcode), allowing faster decoding of simple instructions.
keywords.example: RISC processors use hardwired control because their small instruction set makes it feasible.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 200' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='175' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>RISC</text><text x='120' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Fewer instructions</text><text x='120' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Fewer addressing modes</text><text x='120' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>More registers</text><text x='120' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Fixed-length instructions</text><text x='120' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Hardwired control unit</text><text x='120' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Load/store architecture</text><rect x='270' y='10' width='220' height='175' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CISC</text><text x='380' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Many complex instructions</text><text x='380' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Many addressing modes</text><text x='380' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Fewer registers</text><text x='380' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Variable-length instructions</text><text x='380' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Microprogrammed control</text><text x='380' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Memory-to-memory ops</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-column comparison table: RISC (fewer instructions, fewer addressing modes, more registers, hardwired control) vs CISC (many instructions, many addressing modes, fewer registers, microprogrammed control).

given.aim: Identify all characteristics that distinguish RISC from CISC processors.

terms.meaning: RISC has a small, uniform instruction set (reduced set).
terms.example: MIPS has ~100 instructions vs x86 which has ~1000+.
terms.connects: This is the defining feature of RISC (Reduced = fewer).

terms.meaning: RISC supports only simple addressing modes (register, immediate, load/store).
terms.example: RISC: register indirect only; CISC: direct, indirect, indexed, base+offset, etc.
terms.connects: Simplicity in addressing enables faster decoding.

terms.meaning: RISC compensates for limited memory instructions by providing a large register file.
terms.example: MIPS: 32 registers; typical CISC (8086): 8 general-purpose registers.
terms.connects: More registers reduce memory accesses and enable compiler optimizations.

terms.meaning: RISC's simple, fixed-length instruction set makes hardwired (gate-based) control feasible and faster.
terms.example: CISC uses microcode ROM; RISC uses combinational logic circuits.
terms.connects: Hardwired control is faster and simpler when instruction set is small.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the well-known RISC design principles: simplicity, uniformity, and efficiency.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: fewer instructions
steps.apply: RISC = Reduced Instruction Set Computer. By definition, RISC has a smaller, simpler instruction set than CISC. A is CORRECT.
steps.note: The R in RISC literally stands for Reduced.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: fewer addressing modes
steps.apply: RISC uses only simple addressing modes (typically register and base+offset for load/store). CISC supports complex modes like indexed, auto-increment, etc. B is CORRECT.
steps.note: Fewer addressing modes simplify instruction decoding.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: more registers
steps.apply: RISC processors typically have a large register file (e.g., 32 or more registers) to keep frequently used data in registers and avoid slow memory accesses. C is CORRECT.
steps.note: Large register files are a key RISC feature to compensate for the load/store model.

steps.title: Evaluate option D: hardwired logic
steps.apply: The simplicity and regularity of the RISC instruction set makes hardwired (combinational logic) control units feasible and faster than microprogrammed control used in CISC. D is CORRECT.
steps.note: CISC's complex instructions require microcode; RISC's simple set is directly decoded.

solution.result: All four options A, B, C, D are correct distinguishing characteristics of RISC processors compared to CISC.

questions.formula_note: RISC design principles: small uniform instruction set, few addressing modes, large register file, hardwired control unit, load/store architecture.

questions.question:
Consider the following processor design characteristics:
I. Register-to-register arithmetic operations only
II. Fixed-length instruction format
III. Hardwired control unit

Which of the characteristics above are used in the design of a RISC processor?

A. I and II only
B. II and III only
C. I and III only
D. I, II and III
questions.to_find: Which of the listed characteristics (I, II, III) are used in RISC processor design

understand.plain: RISC processors are designed with certain key features: they operate on registers (not memory directly), use fixed-length instructions for easy decoding, and use hardwired control for speed. This question checks which of three listed characteristics belong to RISC design.

keywords.explain: Arithmetic/logic operations only use register operands (load/store architecture): data must be loaded into registers before computation.
keywords.example: ADD R1, R2, R3 (R1 = R2 + R3) — all operands are registers.

keywords.explain: All instructions are the same size (e.g., 32 bits), which simplifies instruction fetch, decode, and pipelining.
keywords.example: MIPS: every instruction is exactly 32 bits.

keywords.explain: Control signals are generated by combinational logic circuits rather than microcode ROM, making it faster.
keywords.example: RISC uses hardwired; CISC uses microprogrammed (ROM-based) control.

understand.visual_alt: Three RISC characteristics: (I) register-to-register ops only - no memory operands in ALU instructions; (II) fixed 32-bit instruction length enables fast pipelining; (III) hardwired control unit uses logic gates not microcode ROM.

given.aim: Identify which of the three processor characteristics belong to RISC architecture.

terms.meaning: ALU operations work exclusively on register operands (load/store model).
terms.example: No ADD mem, reg — instead: LOAD R1, mem; ADD R2, R1, R3.
terms.connects: This is a core RISC principle that simplifies instruction execution.

terms.meaning: Every instruction occupies the same number of bits, enabling simple and fast instruction fetch.
terms.connects: Fixed length supports efficient pipelining, a hallmark of RISC.

terms.meaning: Control signals derived from combinational logic, not stored microcode.
terms.example: RISC control decodes the fixed-format opcode in one cycle.
terms.connects: Hardwired control is practical for RISC due to its small, uniform instruction set.

given.plan: Verify each characteristic against RISC design principles. All three are canonical RISC features, so the answer is D.

steps.title: Check I: Register-to-register arithmetic
steps.apply: RISC uses a load/store architecture: only LOAD and STORE access memory; all ALU operations use register operands only. Characteristic I is a RISC feature. TRUE.
steps.note: CISC allows memory operands in ALU instructions (e.g., ADD AX, [mem] in x86).

steps.title: Check II: Fixed-length instruction format
steps.apply: RISC instructions are fixed-length (e.g., 32 bits in MIPS/ARM). This simplifies fetch and decode, and is essential for efficient pipelining. Characteristic II is a RISC feature. TRUE.
steps.note: CISC has variable-length instructions (1-15 bytes in x86), complicating decode.

steps.title: Check III: Hardwired control unit
steps.apply: The simple, uniform RISC instruction set makes hardwired (combinational) control units practical and fast. CISC uses microprogrammed control due to complex instruction semantics. Characteristic III is a RISC feature. TRUE.
steps.note: Hardwired control is faster but harder to design for complex ISAs.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: All three characteristics (I, II, III) are RISC design features. The answer is D.
steps.note: This is a standard GATE question testing knowledge of RISC vs CISC features.

solution.result: D. I, II and III — all three characteristics are used in RISC processor design.

questions.formula_note: RISC design principles: register-to-register ALU ops (load/store model), fixed-length instructions for pipeline efficiency, hardwired control unit for speed.

questions.question:
Answer the following questions:
(i) What is cache memory?
(ii) What is the rationale of using cache memory?
questions.answer: Descriptive: Cache memory is a small, fast memory placed between the CPU and main memory that stores recently or frequently used data/instructions. The rationale is the principle of locality (temporal and spatial), which means programs tend to reuse the same data/addresses repeatedly, so keeping them in fast cache reduces average access time significantly.
questions.to_find: Definition of cache memory and the reason (rationale) for using it

understand.plain: Cache memory is a small, very fast memory that sits between the CPU and the slower main (DRAM) memory. It works because programs exhibit 'locality of reference' — they tend to use the same small set of data and instructions repeatedly, so caching them avoids slow main memory accesses most of the time.

keywords.explain: A small, high-speed SRAM memory placed close to the CPU to store copies of frequently accessed data from main memory.
keywords.example: A 256 KB L1 cache holding recently executed instructions, reducing effective memory access time from 100 ns to ~2 ns.

keywords.explain: Programs tend to access a small region of memory repeatedly. Temporal locality: recently accessed data will be accessed again soon. Spatial locality: nearby memory locations will also be accessed.
keywords.example: A loop body is executed thousands of times (temporal); array elements accessed sequentially (spatial).

keywords.explain: Fraction of accesses found in cache; high hit rate (typically 90-99%) means most accesses are fast.
keywords.example: If hit rate = 95%, only 5% of accesses go to slow main memory.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 160' width='420' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='55' width='80' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CPU</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='45' width='100' height='70' class='hi'/><text x='190' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Cache</text><text x='190' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Small, Fast</text><text x='190' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>SRAM ~ns</text><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='290' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='290' y='40' width='120' height='80' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Main Memory</text><text x='350' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Large, Slow</text><text x='350' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>DRAM ~100 ns</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory hierarchy diagram: CPU <-> Cache (small, fast SRAM, ~ns) <-> Main Memory (large, slow DRAM, ~100 ns). Cache acts as a buffer between fast CPU and slow RAM.

given.aim: Define cache memory and explain why it is used in computer systems.

terms.meaning: CPU operates at GHz speeds (~1 ns cycle), while DRAM access takes ~100 ns — a 100x gap.
terms.example: Without cache, CPU would idle waiting for memory on every instruction fetch/data access.
terms.connects: Cache bridges this gap by providing fast storage for active working set.

terms.meaning: A recently accessed memory location is likely to be accessed again soon.
terms.example: Loop counter variable accessed thousands of times during loop execution.
terms.connects: Keeping recently used data in cache exploits temporal locality for speed.

terms.meaning: If a memory location is accessed, nearby locations are likely to be accessed soon.
terms.example: Accessing array[i] likely means array[i+1], array[i+2] will be accessed next.
terms.connects: Cache fetches data in blocks (cache lines), exploiting spatial locality.

given.plan: Define cache as a small fast memory between CPU and RAM, then justify its use through the principle of locality and the CPU-memory speed gap.

steps.title: Define cache memory
steps.apply: Cache memory is a small, high-speed SRAM memory placed between the CPU and main (DRAM) memory in the memory hierarchy. It stores copies of the most recently or frequently accessed instructions and data from main memory. When the CPU requests data, it first checks the cache (cache hit: fast ~1-5 ns); if not found (cache miss), it fetches from main memory (~100 ns) and loads the block into cache.
steps.note: Modern systems have multiple levels: L1 (32 KB, ~1 ns), L2 (256 KB, ~5 ns), L3 (several MB, ~20 ns).

steps.title: Rationale: principle of locality
steps.apply: The rationale is the principle of locality of reference: (a) Temporal locality — recently accessed data will be accessed again soon (e.g., loop variables, frequently called functions). (b) Spatial locality — if address X is accessed, nearby addresses X+1, X+2, ... will soon be accessed (e.g., sequential array traversal). Due to this, a small cache with high hit rate h (90-99%) dramatically reduces average access time: AMAT = h * t_cache + (1-h) * t_main. With h=0.95, t_c=5 ns, t_m=100 ns: AMAT = 0.95*5 + 0.05*100 = 4.75 + 5 = 9.75 ns, much faster than 100 ns.
steps.note: Without locality, cache would not help since every access would be a miss.

solution.result: Cache memory is a small, fast SRAM buffer between CPU and main memory. It is used because programs exhibit locality of reference (temporal and spatial), allowing a small cache to satisfy most accesses quickly and dramatically reduce the average memory access time.

questions.formula_note: AMAT = h * t_cache + (1-h) * t_main; Principle of locality (temporal and spatial) justifies cache effectiveness.

questions.question:
A certain computer system was designed with a cache memory of size 1 Kbytes and main memory size of 32 Kbytes. The cache implementation was fully associative cache with 8 bytes per block. The CPU memory data path was 16 bits and the memory was 2-way interleaved. Each memory read request presents two 16-bit words.

With the model shown below, answer the following questions:
(i) How many bits are needed for addressing the main memory?
(ii) How many bits are needed to represent the TAG, SET and WORD fields?
(iii) Suggest a change in the program size of the model to improve the hit ratio significantly.
questions.answer: Main memory address bits = 15; TAG = 12 bits, SET = 0 bits (fully associative), WORD = 3 bits; Increase the cache size (or program working set to fit in cache) to improve hit ratio.
questions.to_find: Number of bits for main memory address, and the TAG/SET/WORD field breakdown for the fully associative cache

understand.plain: We have a 32 KB main memory, 1 KB cache, 8-byte blocks, fully associative mapping. We need to find how many bits address main memory, and how the address splits into TAG (identifies the block), SET (which cache set — zero for fully associative), and WORD (byte offset within block).

keywords.explain: Any main memory block can be placed in any cache line. There are no sets — only TAG and WORD fields in the address.
keywords.example: For a 4-block cache: a block from any of the 1000 main memory blocks can go into any of the 4 cache lines.

keywords.explain: The unit of data transferred between cache and main memory. Here each block = 8 bytes.
keywords.example: When a miss occurs, 8 bytes are fetched from main memory into one cache line.

keywords.explain: A memory address is split into: TAG (identifies which block is in cache), SET (which cache set to look in), WORD (offset of the byte within the block).
keywords.example: 32 KB main, 8-byte block: 15-bit address = 12-bit TAG + 0-bit SET + 3-bit WORD.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 140' width='500' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='250' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>15-bit Memory Address</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='330' height='45' class='hi'/><text x='175' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>TAG (12 bits)</text><rect x='340' y='35' width='1' height='45' fill='#7F77DD'/><rect x='341' y='35' width='60' height='45' class='bg'/><text x='371' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>SET</text><text x='371' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(0 bits)</text><rect x='401' y='35' width='90' height='45' class='hi'/><text x='446' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>WORD</text><text x='446' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(3 bits)</text><text x='10' y='105' class='sub mut'>Block offset: log_2(8) = 3 bits</text><text x='10' y='122' class='sub mut'>Blocks in main memory: 32K/8 = 4096 = 2^12 => TAG = 12 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 15-bit memory address split into: TAG (12 bits, identifies the memory block) | SET (0 bits, fully associative has no sets) | WORD (3 bits, byte offset within 8-byte block).

given.aim: Determine the memory address bit width and the cache address field sizes for a fully associative cache.

terms.meaning: Total addressable memory is 32768 bytes, requiring 15 bits to address every byte.
terms.connects: 15 bits are needed for the complete memory address.

terms.meaning: Each cache line holds 8 bytes; 3 bits select the byte within a block.
terms.example: Bytes 0-7 in block 0, bytes 8-15 in block 1, etc.

terms.meaning: No SET field exists — any block can map to any cache line. SET bits = 0.
terms.example: Cache has 1 KB / 8 B = 128 lines; any of 4096 main memory blocks can be in any line.
terms.connects: TAG = total address bits - WORD bits - SET bits = 15 - 3 - 0 = 12 bits.

terms.term: Number of blocks in main memory
terms.meaning: 32 KB / 8 B = 4096 = 2^12 blocks; the TAG field must distinguish between all 4096 blocks.
terms.example: TAG = 12 bits to uniquely identify which of the 4096 blocks is in a cache line.

given.plan: Step 1: Compute main memory address bits = log_2(32 KB). Step 2: WORD bits = log_2(block size). Step 3: SET bits = 0 (fully associative). Step 4: TAG = address bits - WORD - SET.

steps.title: Main memory address bits
steps.formula_raw: address bits = log_2(main memory size in bytes)
steps.apply:
Main memory = 32 KB = 32 * 1024 = 32768 = 2^15 bytes.
Address bits = log_2(2^15) = 15 bits.
steps.note: This is the total number of bits in any memory address on this system.

steps.title: WORD field (byte offset within block)
steps.apply:
Block size = 8 bytes = 2^3.
WORD bits = log_2(8) = 3 bits.
steps.note: These 3 bits select which of the 8 bytes within a block is being addressed.

steps.title: SET field (fully associative)
steps.formula_raw: SET bits = log_2(number of sets); fully associative => 1 set => log_2(1) = 0
steps.apply:
In a fully associative cache, all cache lines form a single set (or equivalently, every line is its own 'set'). There is no SET index field.
SET bits = 0.
steps.note: Fully associative = no set partitioning; TAG alone identifies the block.

steps.title: TAG field
steps.formula_raw: TAG bits = total address bits - SET bits - WORD bits
steps.apply:
TAG bits = 15 - 0 - 3 = 12 bits.
Verification: Number of main memory blocks = 32 KB / 8 B = 4096 = 2^12 => 12-bit TAG correctly identifies each block.
steps.note: The TAG is stored alongside each cache line and compared to determine a hit or miss.

steps.title: How to improve hit ratio
steps.apply: The hit ratio depends on the fraction of the program's working set that fits in cache. Since cache = 1 KB and main memory = 32 KB, only 1/32 of the program fits in cache. Suggestions: (a) Increase cache size so more of the working set fits. (b) Restructure the program so its active working set is small enough to fit in cache (e.g., optimize loops for better locality). (c) Increase block size to exploit spatial locality better.
steps.note: Cache size increase is the most direct hardware solution; software loop optimization improves spatial/temporal locality.

solution.result: Main memory address = 15 bits; TAG = 12 bits, SET = 0 bits, WORD = 3 bits. To improve hit ratio: increase cache size or improve program locality.

questions.formula_note: Fully associative cache address breakdown: TAG = log_2(number of blocks in main memory) = address_bits - WORD_bits; SET = 0 for fully associative; WORD = log_2(block_size).
